text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions in the midline and lower abdomen, encountering dense adhesions. After approximately thirty to forty minutes of dissection, the rim of the secondary hernia becomes visible. The surgeon carefully dissects and reduces the incarcerated colon from the hernia sac, pulling on an epithelial appendage to facilitate the reduction.

The surgeon continues dissection at the peristomal site, which is also a hernia, extending the dissection to the lateral sidewall. Adhesions are carefully removed to address obstructive symptoms, ensuring no lead points for obstruction remain. The retro rectus dissection begins on the patient's left side, progressing towards the hernia, with particular attention to the challenging area around the old ostomy site.

The surgeon performs a bilateral transversus abdominis release (TARS) for a seven-centimeter defect, focusing on the ostomy site hernia. Wide mesh reinforcement is planned, with careful preservation of posterior tissue to avoid tension on the visceral sac. The dissection proceeds medially to the ostomy site hernia, extending into the retromuscular space and down to the retropubic space, preparing for the TAR procedure.

The surgeon performs a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR), developing the retromuscular space. The medial aspect of the rectus muscle is visible, with dissection extending towards the neurovascular bundles and the semilunar line. The approach avoids the challenging retro-rectus space near the ostomy site.

The surgeon performs a left-sided transversus abdominis release (TAR), identifying and incising the posterior lamella. Muscle fibers are released as the dissection progresses cephalad towards the preperitoneal fat, moving medially to simplify the procedure. The ostomy site is approached, and some hernia sac tissue is recruited to minimize posterior tension. The TAR results in a loose posterior flap, and the surgeon addresses potential issues from this side before redocking.

The surgeon integrates the anterior fascia and hernia sac at the prior ostomy site to eliminate dead space, followed by placation of a large inception hernia. The focus is on minimizing integration of the linea alba into the placation to reduce potential seroma formation and optimize tissue mesh interface. The procedure concludes with redocking onto the patient's left side.

The surgeon performs a contralateral retromuscular dissection, followed by a transversus abdominis release (TAR). The posterior sheath is released, allowing for significant medialization of the rolled-up rectus muscles. The dissection proceeds smoothly, preparing for posterior closure, defect closure, and mesh placement.

The surgeon performs dissection through the posterior lamella, ensuring protection of the neurovascular bundles, and continues through the muscle down to the tendinous portion of the transversus abdominis. Small fenestrations from prior eight millimeter trocars are visible and will be closed. The loose visceral sac is prepared for easy closure without tension on the posterior sheath. The dissection extends into the retropubic space, revealing a large hernia even after placation.

The surgeon re-approximates the muscle layers and proceeds with placing a 30x30 mesh into the prepared space. A tissue spray is applied for additional fixation to the posterior, and a drain is placed. The mesh is rolled tightly to fit through the eight-millimeter trocars.
